data.title: Automated Generation of Code Contracts

answers.solution_name: Fine-tuned CodeT5/CodeT5+ (for automated code contract generation)

RQa.note: The solution is described in an academic paper published in the proceedings of the GPCE '24 conference.
RQa.evidence: GPCE '24: Proceedings of the 23rd ACM SIGPLAN International Conference on Generative Programming: Concepts and Experiences

RQb.note: The solution is tailored for Java projects, specifically to generate code contracts (pre- and postconditions in JML), making it domain/language-specific.
RQb.evidence: This paper examines the potential of Generative AI to automatically generate code contracts in terms of pre- and postconditions for any Java project without requiring any additional auxiliary artifact.

RQc.note: The approach is based on fine-tuning open-source LLMs (CodeT5 and CodeT5+) and a publicly shared dataset/replication package, indicating an open-source and free usage model.
RQc.evidence: we derive a dataset of more than 14k Java methods... and train the models... Replication Package: Automated Generation of Code Contracts - Generative AI to the Rescue? https://zenodo.org/doi/10.5281/zenodo.13351003

RQd.note: The text only discusses technical challenges (e.g., syntactic validity, semantic correctness of generated contracts), thus the main limitation is technical.
RQd.evidence: We examine the syntactic and semantic validity of the contracts generated for software projects not used in the fine-tuning and find that more than 95% of the generated contracts are syntactically correct and exhibit remarkably high completeness and semantic correctness.

RQe.note: The solution is presented as foundational research that 'sets the stage for future research,' suggesting it is in an experimental phase, not yet production-ready.
RQe.evidence: To this end, our fully automated method sets the stage for future research and eventual broader adoption of Design by Contract in software development practice.

RQf.note: The paper mentions a replication package (which includes a data set), but no explicit reference to a user manual, forum, issue trackers, release notes, or chat community is made.
RQf.evidence: Replication Package: Automated Generation of Code Contracts - Generative AI to the Rescue? https://zenodo.org/doi/10.5281/zenodo.13351003

RQg.note: The solution targets developers, as Design by Contract adoption and the generation of code contracts are concerns of software developers.
RQg.evidence: developers hardly adopt Design by Contract in practice

RQh.note: The text does not specify any industrial case studies or large-scale user studies; it mentions only the evaluation on software projects not used in fine-tuning.
RQh.evidence: We examine the syntactic and semantic validity of the contracts generated for software projects not used in the fine-tuning

IC1.note: The paper focuses on generating code contracts (pre- and postconditions) for Java methods, which is a software construction activity.

IC2.note: It fine-tunes large language models (CodeT5, CodeT5+) to generate code contracts, applying GenAI to a coding-phase activity.

IC3.note: The paper presents a primary study that implements and evaluates the fine-tuned models on a dataset.

IC4.note: A replication package with dataset and scripts is provided, making the solution a usable prototype.

IC5.note: The paper was published in October 2024, which falls within the 2021-2026 window.

EC4.note: The paper is written in English.

EC5.note: This is an original research article, not a secondary or tertiary study.

data.title: Core War: Adversarial Program Evolution

RQa.note: The text is a blog article on emergentmind.com summarizing a research paper about the Digital Red Queen algorithm.
RQa.evidence: Core War is a computational environment and competitive programming game... the Digital Red Queen (DRQ) algorithm...

RQb.note: The solution evolves assembly-like programs (warriors) for the Core War adversarial game, making it specific to that domain and language.
RQb.evidence: Core War is a Turing-complete adversarial game where assembly-like programs, known as warriors, contest control of a virtual machine.

RQc.note: The text does not specify licensing, cost, or access model for the algorithm; it remains unspecified.

RQd.note: The paper acknowledges technical limitations including linear lineage oversimplification, computational expense, and potential bias from behavioral descriptors.
RQd.evidence: Several limitations are acknowledged: The linear lineage (one champion per round) does not fully recapitulate the diversity and concurrency of complex ecosystems. Computational expense may constrain scalability; ... Behavioral descriptor and core parameter choices may bias evolutionary search.

RQe.note: The algorithm is described in a recent (2026) research paper and open questions remain, indicating an experimental stage.
RQe.evidence: domain-agnostic alternatives remain an open question.

RQf.note: No documentation, community forums, issue trackers, release notes, or chat communities are mentioned in the text.

RQg.note: The solution is a research algorithm for adversarial program evolution, targeting AI researchers and those exploring program synthesis, not typical software developers.
RQg.evidence: The DRQ approach positions Core War as a controllable, Turing-complete arena for adversarial program evolution.

RQh.note: The evaluation uses a held-out set of human-designed warriors, not industrial case studies; industry collaboration is not mentioned.
RQh.evidence: Baseline performance is assessed using a held-out set of 317 human-designed warriors.

IC1.note: The solution operates in the adversarial game domain of Core War, not in a software-engineering context.

IC2.note: The activity is adversarial program evolution in a game, not a software-construction activity like coding, testing, or debugging.

IC3.note: The article describes empirical runs and practical implementation of the DRQ algorithm.

IC4.note: No usable prototype or deployed tool is presented; only an algorithmic description and experiments.

IC5.note: The referenced paper was published in 2026, which is between 2021 and 2026.

EC4.note: The text is in English.

EC5.note: The blog is not a survey, review, or systematic mapping.
